en.extract:
The Oriental skylark (Alauda gulgula) is a species of skylark found in the Sino-Indian region and parts of central Asia. Like other skylarks, it is found in open grassland where it feeds on seeds and insects.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Oriental skylark was described by the English soldier and naturalist James Franklin in 1831 and given the binomial name Alauda gulgula. The meaning of the specific epithet gulgula is uncertain but is perhaps a reference to the song as gula is Latin for "throat", or maybe the colour of a gulgula, a sweet which looks like doughnut, made in many parts of Central India.
Other alternate names for the Oriental skylark include small skylark, eastern skylark, Indian skylark and lesser skylark.


Subspecies
Nine subspecies are accepted:

A. g. inconspicua Severtsov, 1873: southern Kazakhstan to eastern Iran, Pakistan and north-western India; originally described as a separate species
A. g. lhamarum Meinertzhagen, R & Meinertzhagen, A, 1926: Pamir Mountains and western Himalayas
A. g. australis Brooks, WE, 1873: south-western India; originally described as a separate species
A. g. inopinata Bianchi, 1905: Tibetan Plateau and north-western China
A. g. vernayi Mayr, 1941: eastern Himalayas and south-western China
A. g. gulgula Franklin, 1831 (syn. A. g. dharmakumarsinhjii, A. g. herberti): north-central India to Sri Lanka and east to Indochina
A. g. weigoldi Hartert, 1922: central and eastern China
A. g. coelivox R. Swinhoe, 1859 (syn. A. g. sala): southern and south-eastern China, Hainan, northern Vietnam; originally described as a separate species
A. g. wattersi R. Swinhoe, 1871 (syn. A. g. wolfei): Taiwan, northern Philippines (Luzon); originally described as a separate species
Most populations are resident, but the northern and northwestern subspecies A. g. inconspicua, A. g. lhamarum, and A. g. inopinata, which breed at high altitudes in areas which get very cold in winter, migrate south and to lower altitudes in the winter. These migratory birds can be found as far west as Israel and northeastern Egypt in winter.


Description
Oriental skylarks range from 15.5–18 cm (6.1–7.1 in) long and 24–30 g (0.85–1.06 oz) weight. They have streaked, yellow-brown upper plumage, with white outer tail feathers and a short crest. Both sexes are similar. The subspecies vary subtly in size, bill length, and plumage tone, with A. g. inopinata being the largest and palest. A. g. coelivox has a longer bill, and A. g. wattersi is more heavily streaked above.
The Oriental skylark differs from the Eurasian skylark in being smaller, with a longer, more pointed bill, and a shorter tail and wings. The outer tail feathers are more buff, and it lacks the white trailing edge to the wings during flight.


Distribution and habitat
This lark breeds across the Sino-Indian region and parts of central Asia.  It winters in the southern parts of its range. 
It occurs from sea level up to 4,300 m altitude, and is usually found in open areas with short vegetation, such as grasslands, marshes, the edges of lakes, etc.  It feeds on seeds and insects.  It avoids forested areas, though it may be found in large forest clearings.


Behaviour and ecology
These skylarks frequently rocket up into the sky, fluttering and singing before descending down to earth. Male Oriental skylarks may also hover in the air and sing, in order to attract a mate.


References


External links
Haryana Online
Birding in Taiwan

The '''Oriental skylark''' ('''''Alauda gulgula''''') is a species of [[Alauda|skylark]] found in the [[Indomalayan realm|Sino-Indian region]] and parts of central Asia. Like other skylarks, it is found in open [[grassland]] where it feeds on [[seed predation|seeds]] and insects.

==Taxonomy and systematics==
The Oriental skylark was [[species description|described]] by the English soldier and naturalist [[James Franklin (naturalist)|James Franklin]] in 1831 and given the [[binomial name]] ''Alauda gulgula''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Franklin | first=James | author-link=James Franklin (naturalist) | year=1831 | title=Catalogue of birds (systematically arranged) which were collected on the Ganges between Calcutta and Benares, and in the Vindhyian hills between the latter place and Gurrah Mundela, on the Nerbudda | journal=Proceedings of the Committee of Science and Correspondence of the Zoological Society of London | language=en, la | volume=Part 1 | issue=10 | pages=114–125 [119] | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12861527 }}</ref> The meaning of the [[specific name (zoology)|specific epithet]] ''gulgula'' is uncertain but is perhaps a reference to the song as ''gula'' is Latin for "throat", or maybe the colour of a [[Gulgula (doughnut)|gulgula]], a sweet which looks like doughnut, made in many parts of Central India.<ref name=hbwkey>{{cite web | last=Jobling | first=J.A. | year=2018 | title= Key to Scientific Names in Ornithology | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | editor4-last=Christie | editor4-first=D.A. | editor5-last=de Juana | editor5-first=E. | work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive | publisher=Lynx Edicions | url=https://www.hbw.com/dictionary/definition/gulgula | access-date=5 August 2018 }}</ref>

Other alternate names for the Oriental skylark include '''small skylark''', '''eastern skylark''', '''Indian skylark''' and '''lesser skylark'''.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=E988F1D549A6773D |title=Alauda gulgula - Avibase|website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org |access-date=2016-12-10}}</ref>

=== Subspecies ===
Nine [[subspecies]] are accepted:<ref name="IOC">{{cite web |title=Nicators, Bearded Reedling, larks – IOC World Bird List |website=IOC World Bird List – Version 15.2 |date=2026-04-13 |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/larks/ |access-date=2026-05-05}}</ref><ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=2026-05-05 }}</ref>
* ''A. g. inconspicua'' <small>[[Nikolai Severtzov|Severtsov]], 1873</small>: southern Kazakhstan to eastern Iran, Pakistan and north-western India; originally described as a separate species
* ''A. g. lhamarum'' <small>[[Richard Meinertzhagen|Meinertzhagen, R]] & [[Annie Meinertzhagen|Meinertzhagen, A]], 1926</small>: [[Pamir Mountains]] and western [[Himalayas]]
* ''A. g. australis'' <small>[[William Edwin Brooks|Brooks, WE]], 1873</small>: south-western India; originally described as a separate species
* ''A. g. inopinata'' <small>[[Valentin Bianchi|Bianchi]], 1905</small>: [[Tibetan Plateau]] and north-western China
* ''A. g. vernayi'' <small>[[Ernst Mayr|Mayr]], 1941</small>: eastern Himalayas and south-western China
* ''A. g. gulgula'' <small>[[James Franklin (naturalist)|Franklin]], 1831</small> (syn. ''A. g. dharmakumarsinhjii'', ''A. g. herberti''): north-central India to Sri Lanka and east to Indochina
* ''A. g. weigoldi'' <small>[[Ernst Hartert|Hartert]], 1922</small>: central and eastern China
* ''A. g. coelivox'' <small>[[Robert Swinhoe|R. Swinhoe]], 1859</small> (syn. ''A. g. sala''): southern and south-eastern China, Hainan, northern Vietnam; originally described as a separate species
* ''A. g. wattersi'' <small>R. Swinhoe, 1871</small> (syn. ''A. g. wolfei''): Taiwan, northern Philippines (Luzon); originally described as a separate species

Most populations are resident, but the northern and northwestern subspecies ''A. g. inconspicua'', ''A. g. lhamarum'', and ''A. g. inopinata'', which breed at high altitudes in areas which get very cold in winter, [[bird migration|migrate]] south and to lower altitudes in the winter. These migratory birds can be found as far west as Israel and northeastern Egypt in winter.<ref name="BoW"/>

==Description==
Oriental skylarks range from {{Convert|15.5|–|18|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and {{cvt|24|–|30|g}} weight. They have streaked, yellow-brown upper [[plumage]], with white outer tail [[feather]]s and a short [[Crest (feathers)|crest]]. Both sexes are similar.<ref name="BoW"/> The subspecies vary subtly in size, bill length, and plumage tone, with ''A. g. inopinata'' being the largest and palest. ''A. g. coelivox'' has a longer bill, and ''A. g. wattersi'' is more heavily streaked above.<ref name="BoW"/>

The Oriental skylark differs from the [[Eurasian skylark]] in being smaller, with a longer, more pointed bill, and a shorter tail and wings. The outer tail feathers are more buff, and it lacks the white trailing edge to the wings during flight.<ref name="BoW"/>

==Distribution and habitat==
This lark breeds across the Sino-Indian region and parts of central Asia.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 December 2023 |title=IOC World Bird List v14.1: Nicators, Bearded Reedling, larks |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/larks/ |access-date=14 April 2024 |website=[[Birds of the World: Recommended English Names|IOC World Bird List]]}}</ref>  It winters in the southern parts of its range. 

It occurs from sea level up to 4,300 m altitude, and is usually found in open areas with short vegetation, such as grasslands, marshes, the edges of lakes, etc.  It feeds on seeds and insects.  It avoids forested areas, though it may be found in large forest clearings.<ref name="BoW">{{Cite web |last=Alström |first=Per |date=24 October 2023 |title=Oriental Skylark ''Alauda gulgula'' |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/orisky1/cur/introduction?login#distriib |access-date=14 April 2024 |website=[[Birds of the World]]}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
These skylarks frequently rocket up into the sky, fluttering and singing before descending down to earth. Male Oriental skylarks may also hover in the air and [[birdcall|sing]], in order to attract a mate.

zh.extract:
小雲雀（學名：Alauda gulgula），属于百靈科雲雀屬，俗名朝天柱、百灵、阿兰、天鹨、阿鹨、告天鸟、大鹨。分布于前苏联、伊朗、阿富汗、巴基斯坦、尼泊尔、印度、缅甸、越南、斯里兰卡、菲律宾、台湾以及中国大陆的青海、甘肃、宁夏、陕西、西藏、四川、贵州、湖南、江西、广西、海南、福建等地，一般栖息于与云雀相似，在离水近的草地原。该物种的模式产地在印度加尔各答和瓦拉纳西之间。
小云雀经常快速冲入天空，展翅高歌之后落地。雄性有时会停在半空中歌唱，吸引伴侣。全長約15公分，上身有黄棕色条纹，白色尾羽，短冠。雌雄外形相似。
台灣有兩種亞種，一為台灣雲雀，二為澎湖雲雀，主要棲息於旱田、草原等較寬廣的地區，海拔300公尺是他們的分布上限，小雲雀在地上築巢，以昆蟲、植物種子為食。


亚种
小云雀华南亚种（Alauda gulgula coelivox）。在中国大陆，分布于云南、贵州、广西、广东、湖南、江西、福建、澎湖群島等地。该物种的模式产地在福建厦门。
小云雀西北亚种（Alauda gulgula inopinata）。分布于尼泊尔、锡金、不丹、印度、缅甸以及中国大陆的西藏、青海、四川、甘肃、陕西等地。该物种的模式产地在青海祁连山及西藏拉萨。
小云雀海南亚种（Alauda gulgula sala）。在中国大陆，分布于广东、海南等地。该物种的模式产地在海南岛海口。
小云雀西南亚种（Alauda gulgula vernayi）。分布于不丹、印度、缅甸以及中国大陆的贵州、四川、云南等地。该物种的模式产地在中国云南与缅甸交界处的Changyinhku。
小云雀台湾亚种（Alauda gulgula wattersi）。分布于菲律宾、台湾等地。该物种的模式产地在台湾南部。
小云雀长江亚种（Alauda gulgula weigoldi）。在中国大陆，分布于四川、陕西、安徽、山东、长江口等地。该物种的模式产地在湖北汉口。


外部連結
小雲雀2，小雲雀4，小雲雀5，小雲雀6，小雲雀7，小雲雀8，小雲雀9。
澎湖小雲雀1 （页面存档备份，存于互联网档案馆），澎湖小雲雀3 （页面存档备份，存于互联网档案馆），澎湖小雲雀4，澎湖小雲雀5，澎湖小雲雀6 （页面存档备份，存于互联网档案馆），澎湖小雲雀7，澎湖小雲雀8，澎湖小雲雀9。
